author: @owhatafeeling
text: Looking online I've noticed that some natural latex rubbers are described as being prevulcanised. Which type would work best for the base? Many thanks in advance!

author: @moumitadas2948
text: Amazing process! one question though, how durable and lond-lasting will this be? Will it be better than plasticol ink?

author: @missnotelf
text: How great is this! I'ld love to try it with pigments I've made from plantmaterial.

author: @josephinejeffery
text: Is the latex liquid when you buy it? Also won’t this block the screen?

author: @mscximenacuriel4491
text: Hello! I can't find the link for the book about natural color :/

author: @emmybee15
text:
Hi Robert,
For my uni project I am working with natural, eco-friendly pigments, binders etc to print onto fabric. I was just wondering if I would be able to use the recipe in this video as part of my project? I would make sure to reference you. If you would rather I didn't then that's no problem! 
Thanks, Emily

author: @selinaal-mukhtar5351
text: Wow! Thank you so much for sharing this... I can't wait to make my own.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers

author: @dallassegno
text: do you have a written recipe?

author: @lightningminded
text: thank you so much, it's been so difficult finding any recipes online muchless purchasable inks.

author: @abdulallam9499
text: Hello Robert was not able to catch of the full name of  Sodium.. used in the base. Also could not catch the name of the binder name. Thanks

replies.author: @wigglyfluffy2330
replies.text:
Sodium alginate
Natural rubber latex

author: @jeliomir
text: Sodium alginate is an organic ingredient?

replies.author: @soberirishman11
replies.text: made from brown algae

replies.author: @soberirishman11
replies.text: or seaweed

author: @petragris1947
text: What a fantastic resource! I'm trying to do screenprinting at home with my kids, and wanted to avoid all the nasty solvents and plastics that go into traditional screenprinting. You have NO idea how much this means to me. Absolutely wonderful! Thank you!

author: @vidneypopples
text: Why did you switch from making 1ltr to making 280 ml halfway through the video? Very confusing!

replies.author: @shengvue9244
replies.text: Confusing to me too.

replies.author: @littlemissabyss9883
replies.text: He made 1 L of the carrier base then only used a smaller amount of the base to combine with the binder

author: @vidneypopples
text: When I add the latex rubber it turns into something resembling scrambled eggs. What am I doing wrong?

author: @vidneypopples
text: What about fixing the dye? Do you add soda ash to make permanent?

author: @vidneypopples
text: What are you saying @ 6:38?

author: @sreejupv4164
text: Hi Robert, Do you know any idea to make biodegradable hydrophobic coating. I am waiting for your reply

author: @iseasurfwear7935
text: OHHH thank you so much, I've just watched how to make the ink base using alginate, I knew seaweed was involved and have been wanting to find screen printing ink with all natural ingredients for years, it's taken until now to look for ways to make it myself as I am changing all my clothing brand to organic and natural. So excited to get experimenting!! Just one thing (for now, I'm sure I'll have more questions) can this be used to screen print onto darker backgrounds, as I currently use a different commercial water based binder for that, but it definitely isn't chemical free so it would be good to know if another ingredient is needed to make it more opaque? Thanks again I'm off to watch more of your video's and buy your book!!

replies.author: @shengvue9244
replies.text: How is your experimentation coming along? I've been looking for a biodegradable ink for a while now and came across this video too. I haven't purchased the materials but just wondering if anyone else has attempt to make it or ran into any issues.

author: @rymdaoues8079
text: Thank you it is so helpful, can i use gomme arabic instead the sodium alginate?

author: @nicolebatson4278
text: Super excited to try this for my art ! One question though, I'm ordering the required ingredients online and everything seems affordable other than the Urea. Are there any other alternatives that I can use to make it cheaper but still functional?

author: @natetravers
text: Hey Robert! This is very cool. I've ordered in some of the materials to try this out. Do you know if the ink will hold fugitive natural dyes very well, or would the dye/pigment need to be colorfast to begin with?

author: @christinaransbury
text: Also I didnt see a link to your book on finding dye colors in nature!  I would love to check it out, thanks!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It's on smash words

author: @christinaransbury
text: should the base be cooled to room temperature before adding the binder?  what should the consistency of the base be?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes and like wallpaper paste

author: @christinaransbury
text:
Looking to source some of this "natural Latex" you speak of.  Any leads?

Also is anyone making fabric inks like this currently?  Having trouble finding people that are making block prints this way.  Any suggestions?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you can get it from eBay - it's just latex rubber - also most art and craft stores have it. I have no idea if anyone else is making it this way sorry - I am a chemist not a printer so i don't know much about the print industry or the field of art prints - again I am sorry for that

replies.author: @christinaransbury
replies.text: Thank you!  I will make my own, very excited.  I got a natural latex rubber from " mold builder" it has ammonia in it, is that still biodegradable?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome - and yes it is

replies.author: @christinaransbury
replies.text: whats the name of your book for foraging for natural dyes?  I just tried the formula, working on trying to get it a lot thicker, its quite watery even after adding a fair amount of the alginate... does it thicken over time while sitting?  or perhaps I just add more alginate....

replies.author: @heidimichels
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith p

author: @insightfool
text: What does the urea do in this formula?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: a cross linker

replies.author: @Kyle-sg4rm
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Could you use Ipomoea alba juice instead of Urea to cross-link? And how might you add it, taking into account the water?
It could be possible to reduce the juice. And maybe some purification would be necessary, so it doesn't effect the overall colour.
Would the Sulfur in the juice produce a white, or less transparent latex than if using Urea to cross-link?
Thanks!

author: @elenitacuchi
text: do you think that is possible to find a substitute to latex?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes the latex is just a flexible binder

replies.author: @elenitacuchi
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith what kind of other binders do you think I could use?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there are too many to go into watch my video on basic ink making

replies.author: @elenitacuchi
replies.text: thank you very much! I love your job!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: u'll have to come and work for us one day lol

author: @AVERAGE_JOE
text: I have been binge watching your ch.  for the last few days and wanted to say thank you for all you do.  I did watch a few videos a few years ago when graphene started and nothing made sence back then but now everything seems to click.  I wasnt ready to hear the greatness. Anyhow ive been a sponge.  This looks like a great way to add graphite to the ink and print on inner layer of a coat or clothing for thermal electric heat.  Thanks again

replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith sounds good. first ill need to make some of your wonderful ink. cheers

author: @FredGandt
text:
A handy thing I picked up during my arguably not so misspent youth, is that to roughly measure 1g of dry powder, get the cap from the end of a Smarties tube, and fill it to level. Approx 1g :-)
We can also make neat little envelopes just the right size for storing 1 or 2 grams of dry powder from sheets from those cubes of square notepaper too, but that's another matter
;-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - that is mispent but amazingly useful - what on earth where you doing to learn such a handy trick lol

replies.author: @FredGandt
replies.text:
Erm .. quite a lot of amphetamine sulphate.
And my shrinks said it might have contributed somewhat to my state of mind :-D

replies.author: @FredGandt
replies.text: P.S. No mistakes or regrets, only opportunities to learn :-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - good man
